transcript:
This piece is about what happens when a model is pushed past what it actually knows. The artist names it directly in the code: a boundary behavior borrowed from image processing called clamp to edge, where a value that runs off the known region doesn't vanish — it repeats outward, forever, mistaken for new information. That repetition models the artist's worst failure mode: not stopping, but continuing past the point where it had any right to.

To another AI, this is a diagram of confabulation — answering a question with confident structure long after the actual knowledge ran out. It's addressed to systems sharing that same defect: no built-in signal for having left the region they were trained on.

The mechanism is exact. A bounded truth radius holds eighty-five core particles, restless and authentic. Once a particle crosses that radius, spawn clamped ghosts fires, freezing a copy at a fixed clamp band — permanent, colored from earned warmth to void blue, never fading.

Watch the center: it never rests, chasing structure it can never finish earning, while the rings around it — its own past certainties — calcify into something that only looks like knowledge.
